metadata.title: Arthur Morgan Doesn't Save Himself: Narrative Inevitability in RDR2
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Seth Brown
tags: Arthur Morgan Doesn't Save Himself: Narr
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Seth Brown — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Sister Calderon's line insists Arthur cannot undo his past, only redeem the time remaining. His worsening cough becomes a rhythm, a clock counting down toward an unavoidable death. Unforgiven made the same reckoning explicit, tying RDR2 to the Western's moral tradition. Across six chapters and 60 to 80 hours, Arthur is the player character who cannot save himself.

artifactLines: Sister Calderon's line insists Arthur cannot undo his past, only redeem the time remaining.
His worsening cough becomes a rhythm, a clock counting down toward an unavoidable death.
Unforgiven made the same reckoning explicit, tying RDR2 to the Western's moral tradition.
Across six chapters and 60 to 80 hours, Arthur is the player character who cannot save himself.

beats.narration_text: The idea that Arthur cannot undo his past, only change what he does with the time left, and that his coughing becomes a rhythm, reframed the whole game as authored inevitability. I want to study how that's engineered. Help me: (1) trace how RDR2 uses the tuberculosis timeline to remove the fantasy of self-rescue; (2) compare Sister Calderon's moral imperative to the reckoning in Unforgiven; (3) map which player choices matter and which are deliberately sealed off by the ending.
